title: Phase 1: Daily Journal MVP
description:
Implement the core daily journaling system - the foundation of Reckon. This creates a TUI for viewing/editing daily journals and CLI commands for scripting.

**Storage Model**:
- Data directory: ~/.reckon/
- Journal files: ~/.reckon/journal/YYYY-MM-DD.md
- SQLite index: ~/.reckon/reckon.db
- Markdown is source of truth; SQLite is rebuildable via rk rebuild

**Journal File Format** (journal/2025-01-15.md):
```markdown
---
date: 2025-01-15
---

## Intentions
- [ ] Open task (unchecked)
- [x] Completed task (checked)
- [>] Carried from previous day

## Wins
- Something accomplished

## Log
- 09:00 Started day
- 10:30 [meeting:standup] 30m
- 14:00 [task:abc123] Working on feature
```

**SQLite Schema**:
```sql
CREATE TABLE journals (date TEXT PRIMARY KEY, file_path TEXT, last_modified INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE intentions (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, journal_date TEXT, text TEXT, status TEXT, carried_from TEXT, position INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE log_entries (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, journal_date TEXT, timestamp TEXT, content TEXT, task_id TEXT, entry_type TEXT, duration_minutes INTEGER, position INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE wins (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, journal_date TEXT, text TEXT, position INTEGER);
```

**Features to Implement**:

1. **Project Setup**
   - Initialize Go module, create directory structure per plan
   - Dependencies: bubbletea, lipgloss, bubbles, cobra, goldmark, fsnotify, xid, modernc.org/sqlite

2. **Config & Paths** (internal/config/)
   - DataDir(), JournalDir(), DatabasePath() helpers
   - Auto-create directories on first run

3. **Domain Models** (internal/journal/)
   - Journal, Intention (open/done/carried), LogEntry (log/meeting/break), Win
   - ID generation using xid

4. **Markdown Parser** (internal/parser/)
   - Parse YAML frontmatter (date)
   - Parse sections by H2 headers (Intentions, Wins, Log)
   - Parse intention markers: [ ], [x], [>]
   - Parse log entries: timestamp, optional [meeting:name], [task:id], duration 30m

5. **Markdown Writer** (internal/parser/)
   - Serialize Journal back to markdown format
   - Round-trip must preserve content

6. **Storage Layer** (internal/storage/)
   - Filesystem: read/write markdown files
   - SQLite: connection with WAL mode, migrations, repository pattern

7. **Journal Service** (internal/journal/service.go)
   - GetToday(), GetByDate(date), AppendLog(content), AddIntention(text), ToggleIntention(id), AddWin(text)
   - Dual-write: update markdown file AND SQLite
   - Auto-carry: when opening a new day, copy open intentions from yesterday as [>]

8. **CLI Commands** (internal/cli/)
   - rk log "msg" - append timestamped entry to today's log
   - rk today - output today's journal to stdout (for LLM piping)
   - rk week - output last 7 days to stdout
   - rk rebuild - delete and regenerate SQLite from all markdown files

9. **TUI** (internal/tui/)
   - Main model with state: currentDate, currentJournal, focusedSection
   - Components: IntentionList (navigable, Enter to toggle), WinsView, LogView, LogInput (text input)
   - Statusbar with keybinding hints
   - Key bindings: Tab (cycle sections), i (add intention), l (add log), w (add win), h/Left (prev day), l/Right (next day, max today), t (jump to today), ? (help), q (quit)
   - Past days are read-only (no editing)

10. **File Watcher** (internal/sync/)
    - Watch ~/.reckon/journal/ for external changes
    - Debounce 100ms, re-index changed files to SQLite
    - Notify TUI to reload if current journal changed

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- rk launches TUI showing today's journal (creates empty if none exists)
- Can navigate sections with Tab, add intentions with 'i', log entries with 'l', wins with 'w'
- Can toggle intentions done with Enter
- Can navigate to previous days with h/l (read-only)
- rk log "test" appends to today's journal from CLI
- rk today outputs journal content to stdout
- rk rebuild regenerates SQLite from markdown files
- External file edits are detected and TUI refreshes
- Opening a new day auto-carries open intentions with [>] marker

**Key Files**:
- cmd/rk/main.go - entry point
- internal/journal/service.go - core business logic
- internal/parser/journal_parser.go - markdown parsing
- internal/tui/model.go - TUI state management

title: Review all logging call sites and tune log levels
description:
Audit every logger.Info/Debug/Warn/Error call site across the codebase and re-tune levels so the default (no --quiet, no explicit --log-level) is not noisy.

Context: reckon-miuh raised the *default floor* from INFO to WARN (and --quiet from WARN to ERROR) in internal/cli/root.go because INFO-level output was too noisy to run at by default -- but that is a blunt instrument: it silences every INFO record uniformly rather than fixing the records themselves. The alternative considered and deferred at the time: a broad sweep re-leveling most existing INFO logs down to DEBUG (reserving INFO for things actually worth seeing at the default level), which would let the default floor come back down to INFO instead of WARN.

Scope:
- Inventory all logger.Info/Debug/Warn/Error call sites (internal/journal and internal/service had ~23 INFO sites as of 2026-07-17, per reckon-miuh codebase-analysis.md; internal/cli currently reachable only via root.go:108 "reckon initialized" -- both journal/service are not yet wired into internal/cli, so this also needs revisiting whichever of those becomes reachable from a CLI command).
- Decide, per site, whether it warrants INFO (worth seeing by default), DEBUG (opt-in via --log-level DEBUG or RECKON_DEBUG), WARN, or ERROR.
- Re-evaluate whether raising the default floor to WARN (reckon-miuh) is still the right call once individual sites are correctly leveled, or whether the default can safely return to INFO with a quieter INFO tier.
- Keep --quiet/--log-level precedence rules from reckon-miuh intact (explicit --log-level wins over --quiet; --quiet wins over LOG_LEVEL/RECKON_DEBUG env).

Acceptance criteria:
- Every logger.* call site reviewed, level either confirmed correct or changed with reasoning.
- Default (no flags) output is not noisy -- reviewer/dogfooder can confirm before/after.
- No regression to reckon-miuh acceptance tests (tests/acceptance/acceptance_test.go TestQuiet_*/TestBaseline_*/TestLogLevel* suite).

title: work-ticket skill: PR-title jq path assumes bd show --json returns object, but it returns array
description:
Phase 9 of .claude/skills/work-ticket/SKILL.md builds PR title via:

  TICKET_TITLE=$(bd show <ticket-id> --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.title // empty')

But `bd show <id> --json` returns a JSON array (`[{...}]`), not a bare object. `.title` on an array errors (`jq: error: Cannot index array with string "title"`), jq exits non-zero, TICKET_TITLE comes back empty, and the fallback `[ -z "$TICKET_TITLE" ] && TICKET_TITLE="<ticket-id>"` fires silently -- PR title ends up as the bare ticket ID twice, e.g. "reckon-miuh (reckon-miuh)" instead of the real title.

Reproduced 2026-07-17 on reckon-miuh (PR #160): title had to be manually corrected post-creation via `gh pr edit`.

Fix: change the jq filter to `.[0].title // empty` (or `.[].title` / `-e` array-aware indexing) to match the actual `bd show --json` shape.

Acceptance: `bd show <id> --json | jq -r '.[0].title // empty'` returns the real title; a fresh work-ticket pipeline run produces a correctly-titled PR without manual correction.
close_reason: Fixed in b046610: jq '.title' -> jq '.[0].title', bd show --json returns array not object

title: rk todo: no delete/remove command for durable todos
description: Durable todos live one-per-file under todos/<ULID>.md but rk todo has no rm/delete subcommand. Removing a mistakenly-created todo requires rm-ing the file by hand and running rk index to reconcile — undiscoverable without knowing the on-disk layout, and easy to leave the index stale if you forget the reindex step. Found while dogfooding (2026-07-16) cleaning up test todos created while probing --quiet behavior. Want: rk todo rm <ref> (or todo done --delete) that removes the file and reindexes in one step.

title: rk todo: no edit command for deadline/scheduled/priority/depends on existing todos
description: rk todo only exposes add/done/list. The only way to mutate an existing durable todo's deadline is the undocumented-from-here rk today act <ref> D <date> — namespaced under 'today', not discoverable via rk todo --help, only found by reading internal/cli/today.go. And today act's key set (t/d/D/p/x/i/c) has no key for depends-on or scheduled at all, so fixing a wrong --depends or adding one after creation has no CLI path — requires hand-editing the todo's frontmatter file. Found while dogfooding (2026-07-16): needed to backfill deadlines onto 3 already-created todos and had to grep source to find the mechanism. Want: rk todo edit <ref> --deadline/--scheduled/--depends/--priority, or extend today act's key set to cover depends-on.

title: rk: --quiet flag doesn't suppress startup INFO log line
description: Every rk invocation prints `time=... level=INFO msg="reckon initialized" ...` to stderr regardless of --quiet. --quiet's help text says "Suppress non-essential output" but only --log-level WARN (or higher) actually silences it. Repro: `rk --quiet todo add "x"` still prints the init log line; `rk --log-level WARN todo add "x"` does not. Found while dogfooding rk for daily task tracking (2026-07-16). Expected: --quiet should suppress this line too, or the flag's docs should clarify it only affects log level, not command output framing.

title: tests/integration_test.go fails to compile on main (journal.NewService signature drift)
description: Pre-existing on main (not caused by reckon-fnqs.1): 'go test -tags integration ./tests/' fails to compile. tests/integration_test.go:49 calls journal.NewService(*Repository, *FileStore, nil) but the function takes 2 args. A surviving-library test left stale by an earlier journal refactor. Fix the call site (verify the intended 3rd arg wasn't dropped, don't blindly delete nil). Confirmed reproducible on clean origin/main.
close_reason: Fixed in commit d6aa1da — integration tests compile and pass

title: work-ticket skill: Phase 7 pattern-extraction sed range never matches actual reviewer output
description:
## Problem
work-ticket skill's Phase 7 pattern-frequency scan (`.claude/skills/work-ticket/SKILL.md`, mirrored in docs/agents/feedback-loop.md:52) scopes its grep to:

    sed -n '/## Functional Review/,/## Design Review/p' "$f"

But the code-reviewer agent's actual output convention (confirmed across all 12 ticket-work/*/review.md files present in this repo, both pre- and post- reckon-scx7's fix) uses per-dimension headers instead: `## Correctness`, `## Architecture`, `## Testing`, `## Maintainability`, `## Error Handling`, `## Performance`, `## Security` (or a numbered `### N. Dimension` variant) -- never a literal `## Functional Review` or `## Design Review` heading.

Result: the sed range matches nothing in every review.md checked, so the pattern-frequency scan silently returns 0 for all 5 tracked patterns on every ticket, always. The scan has never fired since it was introduced -- distinct from reckon-scx7 (closed, PR #143), which fixed the negation-false-positive/monotonic-ratchet/dedup problems in the counting logic itself but did not touch the sed range, which was already broken against real output before and after that fix.

Verified 2026-07-13 while running the work-ticket pipeline for reckon-fnqs.3: scanned all 12 existing ticket-work/*/review.md files, zero matches on any of the 5 patterns, zero `## Functional Review` or `## Design Review` headings found anywhere in the corpus.

## Fix directions (not mandated)
- Update the sed range to match the actual reviewer output headers (e.g. `/^## Summary/,/^## Positive observations/p` or scope to the numbered severity findings directly without a section-range wrapper).
- Or standardize the code-reviewer agent's output template to emit literal `## Functional Review` / `## Design Review` headings so the existing script works as documented.

## Acceptance criteria
- Running the Phase 7 pattern scan against an existing ticket-work/*/review.md with a known Critical/Major/Minor finding in one of the 5 tracked patterns actually increments that pattern's count.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 939395e — sed range removed, grep anchor alone matches both output conventions

title: Triage the pre-pivot backlog + rewrite current_state.md
description:
## Problem
~30 open tickets target v0 code that the v1 consolidation deletes: TUI panel bugs (reckon-cv1a), task-view epics (reckon-1f0h), CLI/TUI parity (reckon-vjd), verb alignment against the v0 verb set (reckon-89hp), notes-browser tickets, colour-palette tickets. Most are obsolete or need re-pointing at the new surface. Working ready-list is currently noise.

Separately, current_state.md (untracked, repo root) documents the pre-pivot architecture -- journal/intentions/wins, DB-primary tasks, the 40-40-18 TUI. It is fiction post-T9.

## Scope
- Walk the open backlog; close what the v0 cut makes moot, re-point what still applies to the v1 verbs/TUI, leave the rest.
- Rewrite current_state.md against the vault/node/index architecture, or delete it in favour of the design docs.
notes: Doc-staleness follow-up from fnqs.1: deleting internal/tui/AGENTS.md left ~10 dangling refs (docs/agents/{planner,implementer,reviewer,test-writer,feedback-loop}.md, docs/TESTING.md, docs/REVIEW_PATTERNS.md, root AGENTS.md). Also internal/cli/AGENTS.md still documents the v0 verb surface as current. Fix during current_state.md rewrite (TUI guide returns with the TUI-rebuild ticket, so decide delete-vs-redirect then).

title: Persistent top-level TUI: static panes over index + verbs
description:
## Problem
rk tui was deleted with the v0 cut because it wrote the stale legacy DB. The full-screen TUI is still wanted -- it just has to be a porcelain, not a second engine.

## Scope (fast path -- v1, this ticket)
- Reads: the SQLite index only (nodes/edges/node_props/aliases/fts views). No direct file walking, no legacy DB.
- Writes: call the same verb functions the CLI calls (addDurableTodo, completeDurableTodoNode, appendLogEntry, ...). The TUI must never open a store the verbs do not own.
- Panes: agenda (rk today's split-actuator model), todos (subject line only, per the subject/body convention), log, notes. Wire the *existing* internal/tui/components (task_list, log_view, notes_pane, schedule_view, wins_view, intention_list, summary_view) directly into a static persistent layout -- do not wait on the Prompt/Wizard abstraction (reckon-fnqs.6).
- Creation/edit flows inside the TUI call verb functions directly (same as a bare CLI invocation), same as v0's per-component helper models did. Not routed through a shared Prompt interface yet -- that's phase 2.
- Salvage what survives from internal/tui/ (layout, keyboard); the model/handlers rebind to the index instead of the legacy journal/service packages.

## Out of scope (moved to reckon-fnqs.10)
Composable board: pluggable pane registry, focus routing, arrange/rearrange panes, Prompt/Wizard-driven creation flows inside panes. Possibly a separate command (e.g. rk board) rather than folded into rk tui.

## Done when
Every TUI mutation is observable as a vault file change and survives rk index --rebuild; no code path in internal/tui imports the journal/service packages; rk tui opens a persistent multi-pane layout on a TTY.

title: Interactive create flows over the v1 verbs
description:
## Want
Keep the mini-TUI entity-creation UX from v0, now over the vault-native verbs.

## Scope
When stdin is a TTY and the verb is invoked bare:
- rk todo add -> wizard: subject -> body (text_editor, multi-line per the subject/body convention) -> scheduled (date_picker) -> deadline -> depends (picker over index rows).
- rk note create -> title -> body -> links.
- rk add -> quick capture bar (text_entry_bar).
With args/flags, behavior is exactly as today: non-interactive, agent-safe.

## Done when
Bare invocation on a TTY opens the wizard and writes the identical file the flag-driven path would; with args, no TUI code is reached.

title: Composable prompt layer: Prompt interface + Wizard host + TTY guard
description:
## Problem
The mini-TUIs are worth keeping (composable-redesign.md:874-878 makes TUI/CLI/MCP peer porcelains over one verb surface), but they are only half-composable today:
- No common interface. task_picker_helper.go / note_picker_helper.go / checklist_run_helper.go each hand-roll a bubbletea host model around one component.
- TaskPicker.Show() takes []journal.Task (task_picker.go:173) -- a legacy domain type that dies with the v0 cut.
- Form (form.go) does text+date fields only; no way to chain form -> picker -> editor as one flow.
- No TTY guard. An agent invoking a verb that pops a mini-TUI hangs forever. Fatal for an agent-first porcelain.

## Scope
- components.Prompt interface: Init/Update/View + Result() T. Every existing component implements it (form, text_editor, date_picker, task_picker, note_picker).
- One generic RunPrompt[T] host replacing the per-component helper models.
- Wizard: chains prompts into one flow, threading a shared result map, ESC-back between steps.
- TTY guard: isatty check + a --no-input flag. Non-tty or --no-input on a verb that would prompt = usage error with the flags to pass instead, never a hang.
- Retarget the pickers at index rows (id/title/type/props) instead of journal.Task.

## Done when
Every component is reachable standalone through one host; a wizard composes at least three of them; a piped/non-tty invocation errors instead of blocking.

title: Multi-line body entry paths for rk todo add / rk add
description:
## Scope
Give the non-interactive (agent + script) surface a way to author a multi-line body, mirroring git:
- -m/--message, repeatable: each -m becomes a paragraph, first is the subject.
- --edit: open $EDITOR on a scratch buffer, use the saved contents.
- body from stdin when the text arg is '-'.

Subject line must be non-empty. The ephemeral inbox stays single-line (it is a checkbox container, todo.go:387).

## Done when
rk todo add -m 'subject' -m 'more detail' produces a todos/<ULID>.md whose first body line is the subject; rk todo list shows only the subject.

title: rk todo show + rk todo edit
description:
## Problem
There is no verb that displays a durable todo's full body -- the gap the subject/body convention creates. rk todo has add/list/done only (todo.go:135).

## Scope
- rk todo show <ref>: full body, props, state, scheduled/deadline, depends-on edges, backlinks. Reuses the ref/alias resolution already in findDurableTodoByRefOrAlias.
- rk todo edit <ref>: span-local body replacement via the node package's surgical splice (never regenerate-from-model).
- Both honor --json.

title: Verb collision: rk import (T9 legacy migrator) vs generic NDJSON feed import
description: PR #155 shipped the one-shot gen-1 migration as `rk import`, but the design record reserves that seam for the durable generic NDJSON node importer (canonical envelope in, alias-keyed upsert, mint-if-absent) that serves external feeds, promotion, and circles — see docs/design/km-architecture-proposal.md §4.11 (blessed pipeline: `jira ... --json | rk-jira-feed | rk import --into feeds/jira`) and composable-redesign.md's generic --import consumer. Decide before anyone builds the jira feed adapter. Options: (a) move the one-shot migrator under the existing `rk migrate` parent (e.g. `rk migrate legacy`), freeing `import` for the durable verb; (b) name the feed consumer `rk ingest` and leave the migrator as-is. Option (a) preferred: the migrator is one-shot and `migrate` already exists; `import` should belong to the long-lived seam. Constraint either way: the feed consumer must be generic (no jira-specific logic in core) per the §4.11 founding constraint (read-only projection; reckon never writes back).

title: work-ticket skill: feedback-loop pattern counter is a monotonic ratchet with false positives
description:
Found via fable-model critique of .claude/skills/work-ticket/SKILL.md (2026-07-02), verified live.

Phase 7 (~line 314-318):
  for pattern in "unwrapped error" "missing defer" "closure capture" "nil check" "missing validation"; do
    count=$(grep -irl "$pattern" .claude/work/*/review.md 2>/dev/null | wc -l)

Three confirmed problems:
1. Negation false-positive (tested): a review reading 'Checked for unwrapped error handling — none found, all errors properly wrapped' still matches grep -irl "unwrapped error". The counter cannot distinguish 'found this problem' from 'explicitly verified absent'.
2. Already primed: docs/REVIEW_PATTERNS.md currently has 9 case-insensitive hits across these exact 5 pattern strings (grep -icE confirmed) -- the counter's food source already exists in the repo, this is not a someday risk.
3. Monotonic, no dedup: grep -irl | wc -l counts cumulatively across every .claude/work/*/review.md ever produced (once reckon-kqxz is fixed and these actually persist). Nothing decrements/resets/dedups. Once a pattern crosses a threshold, EVERY subsequent ticket re-triggers the same doc-append action with no idempotency check described -- 'add to AGENTS.md Common Pitfalls' could fire repeatedly for the same pattern.
4. Fixed vocabulary of exactly 5 strings -- any novel recurring review finding never enters the loop regardless of frequency. The mechanism can only reinforce patterns it was told about in advance, contradicting the stated intent of a self-improving feedback loop.

Fix directions (not mandated, implementer's call):
- Require the grep match to be near an affirmative marker (e.g. a review.md structured format with explicit PASS/FAIL per pattern, not free-text grep) to kill the negation false-positive.
- Track already-actioned (pattern, threshold) pairs (e.g. in .claude/metrics/) so crossing a threshold triggers the doc-append exactly once, not on every future ticket.
- Either expand pattern discovery beyond the fixed 5 (e.g. review.md structured findings with a category field) or explicitly document that the loop only reinforces a curated list and rename/scope the feature accordingly.

Acceptance criteria:
- A review explicitly stating a pattern was checked and NOT found does not increment that pattern's count.
- Crossing a threshold for a given pattern triggers its doc-append action at most once, not on every subsequent ticket.
- The counting mechanism (or its documented scope) no longer silently caps at exactly 5 hardcoded patterns without acknowledging that limitation.

title: work-ticket skill: Haiku preflight asked to do codegen (insert defer), not just report
description:
Found via fable-model critique of .claude/skills/work-ticket/SKILL.md (2026-07-02).

Phase 5 (~line 267-276) assigns Haiku 4.5 (chosen for 'mechanical checks, 10-20x cheaper') both detection AND an 'On FAIL: Fix mechanical issues automatically (formatting, missing defer)' step. Inserting a missing 'defer' statement is not mechanical -- correct placement requires understanding control flow and resource lifetime; wrong placement introduces real bugs, applied by the cheapest/least-capable model in the pipeline, AFTER the point where a human or Opus reviewer would normally catch it (Phase 6 review comes after preflight).

Separately: the preceding one-liner 'git diff --exit-code || git add -A && git commit -m "fix: go fmt..."' parses as (diff-check || add) && commit -- on a clean diff (nothing to fix) this still attempts a commit with nothing staged, producing a confusing error for Haiku to interpret.

Fix: Haiku preflight reports findings only (including 'missing defer at X'); any actual code fix (defer insertion, non-fmt fixes) routes to Sonnet (the Phase 4 model), not Haiku. Fix the shell one-liner's fmt-commit logic to only commit when go fmt actually changed something.

Acceptance criteria:
- Haiku preflight never writes non-fmt code changes.
- The auto-fix path for anything beyond 'go fmt' output routes through a capable-enough model.
- The fmt-commit shell logic does not attempt an empty commit on a clean diff.

title: work-ticket skill: stall detection defeated by unnormalized go test output (timings)
description:
Found via fable-model critique of .claude/skills/work-ticket/SKILL.md (2026-07-02).

Phase 4 (~line 216-225) detects a stalled implementer by diffing consecutive raw 'go test ./...' outputs across iterations, escalating after 3 identical outputs. But go test output embeds per-test/per-package timings (e.g. '--- FAIL: TestX (0.01s)', 'ok pkg 0.42s'), so two consecutive runs are almost never byte-identical even when the implementer is genuinely stuck making no progress. The safety valve that makes a 10-iteration Sonnet budget affordable is dead as specified -- expect full 10-iteration burns on stuck tickets instead of a 3-iteration escalation. Same defect affects state.json's last_test_output_hash.

Fix: normalize test output before comparing/hashing -- strip timing suffixes and sort lines (or use 'go test -json' and compare only PASS/FAIL/test-name fields, ignoring Elapsed).

Acceptance criteria:
- Two consecutive genuinely-stuck iterations (same failures, different timings) are correctly detected as a stall within 3 iterations.
- A genuinely-progressing iteration (different failures) is never falsely flagged as stalled.

title: v1: sanctioned FTS5 MATCH surface for rk query
description: rk query (reckon-p0zs / PR #140) defers full-text search. The 'fts' object in the T2 index is a VIEW exposing columns only (id, body); FTS5 MATCH requires the private '_fts' virtual table, which is outside the public stable-view contract. The query command currently REJECTS any MATCH with a clear 'not supported in v1' error. Decide and implement the sanctioned full-text surface: either (a) a public MATCH-capable view/function over _fts, (b) a dedicated rk query --fts/--search flag that targets _fts internally, or (c) promote a stable fts contract. Must preserve the read-only + stable-view-only guarantees.
design: Options: (a) public vtable-backed view; (b) --search flag bridging to _fts; (c) stable fts MATCH contract. Constraint: keep read-only enforcement and avoid exposing private _-tables directly to user SQL.
notes: PR #141: https://github.com/MikeBiancalana/reckon/pull/141 — public fts_search vtable MATCH surface. Awaiting merge.
close_reason: Merged PR #141 (squash 63b00d5): public fts_search vtable MATCH surface

title: Review M-parser-scope: enumerate supported frontmatter/markdown subset + real-Obsidian tests
description: From foundation-review-2026-06-24 (parser-scope). Raw stays byte-safe but the DERIVED view (hence the index) is wrong for shapes Obsidian emits: block-style YAML (aliases:\n  - a) is invisible; CRLF files fail the ---\n prefix so all typed fields go empty; inline code `[[x]]` is linkified into spurious references edges; multi-target ref props (depends: [[A]], [[B]]) are dropped. Done when: the supported frontmatter/markdown subset is enumerated in node package docs; block-scalar frontmatter (lists), CRLF, inline-code-inert, and multi-target ref props are handled (or explicitly+loudly unsupported); tests cover the real Obsidian shapes since the same vault is edited by Obsidian. Land before T8 (linking), ideally before T4.

title: Review M2: ULID mint policy + rk adopt for id-less (Obsidian) files
description: From foundation-review-2026-06-24 (M2). Policy (recommended): the index NEVER mutates truth files, so id-less files key on file:<relpath> and are not rename-stable until a tool touches them. Tools that CREATE nodes mint a ULID via node.NewNode/Render (H1). For Obsidian-authored id-less files the user wants first-class, provide an explicit opt-in rk adopt that stamps ULIDs into them (user-initiated mutation, never an automatic index side effect). Done when: the policy is recorded in the design doc; rk adopt [path...] stamps id: into id-less files via span-safe write (frontmatter insert) and is tested; mint-on-create is wired in the tool create paths.

title: v1-T9: migration of current DB-primary data to text-truth
description: The long pole (watch-item #4), beside the keystone. One-shot importer: current SQLite-primary tasks/notes/checklists -> text-truth nodes; xid -> ULID with the old xid kept as an alias (so existing references resolve); journal markdown -> log nodes; verify pass. Done when: migrating a COPY of real data yields matching counts; spot-checked nodes round-trip; old slug/id links resolve via alias; a dry-run + verify mode exists; tested on sample data.

title: v1-T8: note tool (rk note) + linking
description: The zettelkasten pillar. rk note create/show; file-per-item note nodes; body [[wikilinks]] -> reference edges; frontmatter typed-edge props -> typed edges; aliases/slugs (Obsidian-compatible, global flat namespace, index-enforced uniqueness); dangling links allowed, stored as unresolved edges, auto-resolve on target creation, queryable. Done when: notes create and round-trip; forward links + backlinks (index-derived) are queryable; a dangling link resolves once its target exists; rename retains the old alias as a redirect; tested. FILENAME POLICY (settled 2026-07-09, km-architecture-proposal.md §4.2): note filename = title-derived slug (the canonical alias), NOT the ULID — notes are the browse-heavy type; identity stays inline (id: wins) so rename is free and slug churn rides the alias-redirect machinery. Raw [[ULID]] links may dangle in Obsidian (reckon resolver handles them); per-note escape hatch = list the ULID in aliases:. Also adopt the T8 frontmatter conventions from the proposal: title + description (mandatory-by-convention), optional stage: seedling|budding|evergreen, no timestamp/updated field (git is the versioning backplane; OKF timestamp derived at export). Generate notes/**/index.md (OKF progressive disclosure) as part of reconcile or rk note index.

title: v1-T7: rk today (split-actuator agenda)
description: The org-agenda flow that fixes the Logseq query->schedule->do gap. Query overdue + scheduled-today + today-pinned (+ reminders later); in-list relative scheduling keys (t/d/D/p) and do keys (x/i/c) actuating NATIVE nodes via span-local writes; completion emits a linked log-entry (did->task). SPLIT actuator: externally-fed work tickets are read-only with an open-in-source jump (feed stubbed in v1). Done when: the agenda surfaces the correct set; actuation writes through to files and reindexes; completion logs a did entry; native rows actuate while stubbed external rows are read-only; tested.

title: v1-T6: recurrence via stored scheduled cursor (org-style)
description: Recurring todos without log-derived fragility. repeat: org repeaters (+Nd fixed, ++Nd skip-to-future, .+Nd from-completion); on completion advance the rule node scheduled: prop via span-local edit AND write a did log entry as audit; surface the current occurrence; pile-up materializes an ephemeral instance. Cursor lives in text (frontmatter), so it survives index rebuild. Done when: a recurring todo advances correctly for each repeater family on completion; a did entry is written; blowing away+rebuilding the index preserves the cursor; tested.

title: v1-T5: todo tool (rk todo) durable + ephemeral
description: rk todo add/list/done. Durable todo = file-per-item node (its own ULID, file); ephemeral todo = light property/group-file (no stable address, per the ephemeral/durable split); props state/scheduled/deadline; depends: frontmatter prop -> depends-on edges; state changes via span-local edits. Done when: create/list/complete works; todo files round-trip; depends-on edges show in the index; the ephemeral-vs-durable distinction is queryable; tested.

title: Composable redesign — mechanics phase (index schema, per-tool specs, resolver)
description:
Design phase for the UNIX-composable reckon redesign is COMPLETE. Full record + reasoning + decision log: docs/design/composable-redesign.md (read the 'Status & resuming (start here)' section first).

Decided pillars: plain-text files (Obsidian-flavored markdown) + git substrate; file (storage) vs node (address) atoms; ULID inline type-agnostic identity + #frag block IDs + human aliases; graph-query over a property-graph index in SQLite (per-device, never synced); pipe + emit-side disposition (--ref/--pop) + generic --import for promotion; canonical node (per-tool parse/serialize) as keystone; Go packages + one multi-call rk binary + rk-<name> PATH extensions.

Next (mechanics, all downhill from the canonical node):
1. Index schema — SQLite nodes/edges/fts/aliases; per-device, regenerated, never synced.
2. Per-tool specs — props, file layout, parse/serialize, opinionated structure per tool.
3. Resolver — alias->ULID, [[ref]]->file, ULID#frag->span.
4. query->schedule->do UX (Logseq's weak spot; a UX requirement, not just data).
5. Migration — map current reckon (per-type tables, xid, note_links) onto unified node + edges graph; salvage parser/storage/TUI.

Core is domain-agnostic: a work-context agent can flesh out new facets as a new tool/package or an rk-<name> PATH extension without touching core, as long as it speaks the canonical node format.
notes: POST-ASSESSMENT (2026-06-22): see docs/design/composable-redesign-rebuttal.md + the '2026-06-22 amendments' + 'Lean v1 scope' sections of composable-redesign.md. Key changes: (1) parse/serialize round-trip is now GATING (span-local write-back + parse(serialize(parse(f)))==f fuzz gate on real Obsidian/Logseq input) before any truth write; (2) reuse estimate corrected ~60% -> ~20-35%, tasks/notes/checklists are DB-primary today, truth-inversion is the long pole; (3) author field added to canonical node; (4) recurrence = stored scheduled cursor (org-style), NOT log-derived; (5) synthesis = reserved rk-brief extension seam, NOT core; rk today = split actuator; work Jira/GH reconcile lives in rk-jira/rk-gh extensions; (6) concurrency downgraded, merge=union+sort+dedup on log. Build the Lean v1 scope first.
close_reason: Cleanup 2026-06-24: stale in_progress / decomposed. 53fu decomposed into v1-T* + review tickets (see docs/design/composable-redesign.md). 4u1c#136, elfb#133, obed#134, pjjr#132, gcuu#135 merged to main.

title: Allow logs for the day without a timestamp in a separate section
description:
## Problem
Currently, all log entries require a timestamp. There's no way to add general notes or context for the day that don't fit into a specific time slot (e.g., "Working from home today", "Feeling productive", "Goals for today").

## Solution
Support timestamp-less (atemporal) log entries that appear above timestamped logs in the Logs section with soft visual separation.

## User Experience

### In TUI
```
━━ Logs ━━

Working from home today
Remember to follow up with client

─────────────────────────  (soft divider)

09:30  Started work on feature X
10:45  Meeting with team
```

### In Markdown
```markdown
## Logs

Working from home today
Remember to follow up with client

- 09:30 Started work on feature X
- 10:45 Meeting with team
```

### In `rk today` Output
Same format as TUI - atemporal logs at top, soft divider, then timestamped logs.

## Implementation Details

### Data Model Changes
`internal/journal/models.go`:
- `LogEntry.Timestamp` should be optional (use pointer: `*time.Time`)
- When `Timestamp == nil`, the entry is atemporal
- Atemporal entries still have `Position` field for ordering among themselves

### Parser Changes
`internal/journal/parser.go`:
- Recognize log entries without timestamps (plain lines in Logs section)
- Differentiate from bulleted timestamp entries (`- HH:MM content`)
- Parse order determines position for atemporal entries

### Writer Changes
`internal/journal/writer.go`:
- Write atemporal entries as plain lines (no bullet, no timestamp)
- Write timestamped entries with `- HH:MM` format
- Maintain separation (blank line) between atemporal and timestamped sections

### TUI Changes
`internal/tui/components/log_view.go`:
- Separate atemporal and timestamped logs in rendering
- Display atemporal logs first, in order
- Add soft visual divider (e.g., thin line with subtle color)
- Display timestamped logs after divider, sorted by time

### CLI Changes
`internal/cli/log.go`:
- `rk log add` without timestamp creates atemporal entry
- `rk log add` with timestamp creates timestamped entry
- Consider flag: `rk log add --time=10:30` vs `rk log add --note` (atemporal)

## Example Usage

```bash
# Atemporal log
rk log add "Working from home today"

# Timestamped log (current behavior)
rk log add "Started on feature X"  # Uses current time
rk log add "Meeting notes" --time=14:00
```

## Ordering Behavior
- Atemporal logs: Ordered by `Position` field (order they appear in file)
- Timestamped logs: Ordered by `Timestamp` (chronological)
- Atemporal section always appears above timestamped section

## Migration
- Existing logs (all timestamped) work unchanged
- New atemporal logs can be added to existing journals
- No data migration needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can create atemporal log entries (no timestamp)
- [ ] Atemporal logs appear above timestamped logs in TUI
- [ ] Soft visual divider separates atemporal from timestamped
- [ ] Atemporal logs maintain order from markdown file
- [ ] Parser correctly reads atemporal entries from markdown
- [ ] Writer correctly writes atemporal entries without timestamps
- [ ] `rk log add` supports creating atemporal entries
- [ ] `rk today` output shows atemporal logs correctly
- [ ] Existing timestamped logs continue to work unchanged

## Visual Design (Soft Divider)
Options for soft visual separation:
1. Blank line + subtle horizontal rule (─── in dim color)
2. Just a blank line
3. Thin dotted line (· · · in dim color)

Prefer option 1 - clear but not intrusive.

title: Align subcommand verbs on a consistent and obvious set
description:
## Problem
Subcommand verbs are inconsistent across different resource types in the CLI:
- `task` uses: **new**, list, show, edit, delete, done, schedule, deadline, log, note
- `notes` uses: **create**, edit, show
- `log` uses: **add**, delete, note
- `schedule` uses: **add**, list, delete
- `win` uses: **add**, list, delete

This creates a poor UX where users must remember different verbs for similar operations on different resources.

## Solution
Standardize on the verb set: **add, list, show, edit, delete** across all resource types.

## Renaming Map

### task
- `rk task new` → `rk task add`
- Keep: list, show, edit, delete
- Special: done, schedule, deadline, log, note (resource-specific actions)

### notes
- `rk notes create` → `rk notes add`
- **Add**: `rk notes list` (or use search from reckon-pfpb)
- Keep: show, edit
- **Add**: `rk notes delete`

### log
- Keep: add, delete, note
- **Add**: `rk log list` (show log entries for a date)
- **Add**: `rk log show` (show a specific log entry with details)
- **Add**: `rk log edit` (edit a log entry)

### schedule  
- Keep: add, list, delete
- **Add**: `rk schedule show` (show details of a specific schedule item)
- **Add**: `rk schedule edit` (edit a schedule item)

### win
- Keep: add, list, delete
- **Add**: `rk win show` (show a specific win)
- **Add**: `rk win edit` (edit a win)

## Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Add New Commands (Backward Compatible)
1. Add new verb commands alongside old ones
2. Mark old verbs as deprecated in help text
3. Both work, but new verbs are documented

### Phase 2: Deprecation Warning
1. Print deprecation warnings when old verbs are used
2. Update all documentation to use new verbs
3. Update bash completion

### Phase 3: Removal (Breaking Change)
1. Remove old verb commands in a major version bump
2. Update CHANGELOG with migration guide

## Files to Modify
- `internal/cli/task.go` - Rename `taskNewCmd` to `taskAddCmd`
- `internal/cli/notes.go` - Rename `notesCreateCmd` to `notesAddCmd`, add list/delete
- `internal/cli/log.go` - Add list, show, edit commands
- `internal/cli/schedule.go` - Add show, edit commands
- `internal/cli/win.go` - Add show, edit commands
- `internal/cli/root.go` - Update command registration
- `README.md` - Update all examples
- Bash completion script (if exists)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All resource types support: add, list, show, edit, delete (where applicable)
- [ ] Old verb names are deprecated with warnings
- [ ] Help text uses new verbs exclusively
- [ ] Documentation updated with new verbs
- [ ] Bash completion updated
- [ ] CHANGELOG documents the breaking change
- [ ] Migration path is clear for users

## Notes
- Resource-specific verbs like `task done`, `task schedule`, `task deadline`, `task note`, `log note` should remain as they are domain-specific operations, not CRUD
- Consider adding aliases for common operations (e.g., `rk task done` could also be `rk task complete`)

title: No way to view zettelkasten notes in the TUI
description:
## Problem
Zettelkasten notes can only be interacted with via CLI commands (`rk notes create/edit/show/search`). There is no way to view, navigate, or edit notes within the main TUI or a dedicated TUI mode.

## Solution
Create a separate TUI mode for browsing and managing zettelkasten notes, similar to how `rk` (no args) launches the journal TUI.

## Proposed Command
```bash
rk notes           # Launch notes TUI (instead of showing help)
rk notes tui       # Explicit TUI launch (alternative)
```

## TUI Features

### Layout
- **Left pane**: List of notes (title, tags, date)
  - Fuzzy search/filter at top
  - Sort options: recent, alphabetical, by tag
- **Right pane**: Selected note content
  - Show title, metadata (tags, dates)
  - Render markdown content
  - Display links (outgoing and backlinks)

### Navigation
- `j/k` or arrows: Navigate note list
- `/`: Focus search/filter box
- `Enter`: Open note in detail view (or expand in place)
- `e`: Edit selected note (open in $EDITOR)
- `n`: Create new note (launch creation form)
- `d`: Delete selected note (with confirmation)
- `t`: Filter by tag
- `q`: Quit TUI

### Advanced Features (future considerations)
- Follow links between notes (navigate graph)
- Tag management (add/remove tags without editing file)
- Visualize note connections
- Show orphaned notes (no links in or out)

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
- `internal/tui/notes/` - New package for notes TUI
- `internal/tui/notes/model.go` - Main TUI model
- `internal/tui/notes/components/` - Note-specific components

### Files to Modify
- `internal/cli/notes.go` - Make `notes` launch TUI by default when no subcommand
- `cmd/rk/main.go` - Wire up notes TUI command

### Components Needed
- `NoteList` - Filterable/searchable list of notes
- `NoteDetailView` - Render note content, metadata, links
- `NoteSearchBar` - Fuzzy search input
- `TagFilter` - Tag-based filtering UI

### Data Access
- Reuse existing `NotesService` from `internal/service/`
- May need to add methods for:
  - Real-time file watching (for external edits)
  - Filtering/searching notes
  - Batch operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `rk notes` (no subcommand) launches the TUI
- [ ] Can navigate and view all notes in the TUI
- [ ] Can search/filter notes by title and tags
- [ ] Can view note content, metadata, and links
- [ ] Can edit notes (opens $EDITOR)
- [ ] Can create new notes from TUI
- [ ] Can delete notes with confirmation
- [ ] Navigation is smooth and intuitive
- [ ] TUI reflects changes made externally (file watcher)

title: Task selection still goes through hidden done tasks
description:
## Problem
In the TUI task list, when navigating with keyboard (j/k or arrows), the cursor moves through done tasks even though they are hidden from view. This creates a confusing UX where the selection appears to skip over visible tasks or get stuck on invisible items.

## Root Cause
The `GroupTasksByTime` function (internal/tui/components/task_list.go:539) filters done tasks from display using `if task.Status == journal.TaskDone { continue }`, but these tasks may still be present in the underlying list model, allowing cursor navigation to select them.

## Solution
1. Add a toggle keybinding (suggest 'h' for hide/show) to show/hide done tasks
2. When done tasks are hidden (default state), completely exclude them from the list model - not just from rendering
3. When shown, include done tasks in the list model so they can be selected
4. Persist the hide/show preference (either in memory for the session or in config)

## Implementation Details
- Modify `GroupTasksByTime` or the list building logic to conditionally include/exclude done tasks based on toggle state
- Update `TaskList.UpdateTasks()` to respect the toggle state when rebuilding items
- Add keybinding handler in the task list Update method
- Ensure cursor position is preserved when toggling (select nearest visible task)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When done tasks are hidden, cursor navigation only moves through open tasks
- [ ] Toggle keybinding switches between showing and hiding done tasks
- [ ] Cursor position is sensibly preserved when toggling
- [ ] Visual indicator shows current hide/show state (e.g., in help text or status bar)

title: TUI: Top border lines of panels not visible
description:
## Bug Description
The top border lines of TUI panels are not visible in the rendered output. Panel headers (titles) are visible, but the box-drawing characters that should form the top edge of each panel (┌────┐ style) are cut off or not being rendered.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Launch `rk` TUI
2. Observe the top of each panel section
3. Compare with expected box-drawing border layout

## Expected Behavior
Each panel should have complete borders:
- Top border: `┌────────┐` 
- Side borders: `│` (left and right)
- Bottom border: `└────────┘`
- Headers should appear BELOW the top border line within the box

## Actual Behavior
- Panel headers ("Log Entries", "Schedule", "Intentions", "Wins") are visible
- Side borders (│) are visible
- Bottom borders (└────┘) are visible
- Top borders (┌────┐) are NOT visible - appear to be cut off

## Visual Evidence
Confirmed via gotty + Playwright MCP visual inspection:
- All screenshots show panel headers at the very top of visible area
- No top border line visible above headers
- Side and bottom borders render correctly
- Suggests either:
  - Top line of viewport is being cut off
  - Panels are positioned 1 line too high
  - Top border is not being drawn

## Affected Panels
- Left panel: "Log Entries"
- Center panel: Task sections (TODAY, THIS WEEK, ALL TASKS)
- Right panels: "Schedule", "Intentions", "Wins"

## Impact
- Visual inconsistency - incomplete panel borders
- Makes layout look unpolished/broken
- May indicate underlying layout calculation bug
- Could affect terminal size detection or viewport positioning

## Possible Causes
1. Terminal viewport offset calculation is off by one line
2. Panel rendering starts at row 0 instead of row 1
3. Top border being drawn outside visible terminal area
4. Bubbletea View() method not accounting for top margin
5. Alt screen buffer positioning issue

## Files Likely Involved
- internal/tui/model.go (View() method, layout calculations)
- internal/tui/components/task_list.go (task panel rendering)
- internal/tui/components/log_view.go (log panel rendering)
- Any component that renders panels with borders

## Related Issues
- May be related to overall layout/rendering system
- Similar to selection indicator issue (reckon-dgrp) in that it's a visual rendering problem

title: TUI: No visual selection indicator in ALL TASKS section
description:
## Bug Description
When navigating tasks in the TUI, there is no visual selection indicator (highlighting, cursor, or color change) on tasks in the ALL TASKS section. This makes it impossible to see which task is currently selected when using keyboard navigation.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Launch `rk` TUI
2. Press Tab to switch to Tasks section (status bar shows "Tasks |")
3. Press 'j' or 'k' to navigate through tasks
4. Observe the ALL TASKS section

## Expected Behavior
- The currently selected task should have a visual indicator (e.g., highlighted background, different text color, cursor symbol, border change)
- Pressing 'j'/'k' should visibly move the selection indicator between tasks
- The selected task should be clearly distinguishable from unselected tasks

## Actual Behavior
- No visual change occurs when pressing navigation keys
- All tasks appear identical regardless of navigation input
- Cannot determine which task is currently selected
- Pressing Space/Enter produces no visible effect (can't tell if a task is being toggled/expanded)

## What Works
- Tab key successfully switches between sections (status bar updates correctly)
- Keyboard input is being received (e.g., pressing 't' to add task works)
- Status bar shows correct section name and keybindings
- Other sections (TODAY, THIS WEEK) may have same issue but are empty in test

## What Doesn't Work
- No visual feedback for task selection in ALL TASKS section
- Navigation keys ('j'/'k') don't produce visible selection changes
- Action keys (Space, Enter) don't show which task they're operating on

## Testing Evidence
Tested using gotty + Playwright MCP for visual inspection:
- Pressed 'j' multiple times - no visual change
- Pressed Tab - section switched correctly (status bar updated)
- Pressed Space - no visible task toggle
- Pressed Enter - no visible expansion

## Possible Causes
1. Missing selection styling/highlighting in task list rendering code
2. Selection state not being applied to the rendered task items
3. Focus not properly moving to ALL TASKS section items
4. Selection indicator being rendered but invisible (color issue)

## Impact
- Makes task navigation unusable - cannot see which task you're selecting
- Cannot effectively use keyboard shortcuts on specific tasks
- Significantly degrades TUI usability for task management

## Files Likely Involved
- internal/tui/components/task_list.go
- internal/tui/keyboard.go (navigation handlers)
- internal/tui/model.go (selection state)
close_reason: Implemented visual selection indicator for TUI task sections. Fixed renderTaskSection to apply SelectedStyle to selected tasks. Added comprehensive tests. Code reviewed and approved as production-ready.

title: Refactor large TUI Update function into handler methods
description:
## Overview
Refactor the 508-line `Update()` function in `internal/tui/model.go` into smaller, focused handler methods organized by message type. This will improve code readability, testability, and maintainability while preserving all existing functionality.

## Background Context
- The Update() function is the core event handler in Bubbletea TUI applications
- Currently handles 20+ message types in a single 508-line function
- Message types include: keyboard events, window size changes, journal loading, task operations, etc.
- Refactoring should follow Bubbletea best practices while maintaining backward compatibility
- No behavioral changes - pure refactoring for code quality

## Current State Analysis

**File**: `internal/tui/model.go:237-744` (508 lines)

**Message types handled**:
- `tea.WindowSizeMsg` - Terminal resize
- `journalLoadedMsg` - Journal data loaded
- `journalUpdatedMsg` - Journal updated
- `tasksLoadedMsg` - Tasks loaded
- `components.TaskToggleMsg` - Task toggled
- `components.DeleteTaskMsg` - Task deleted
- `components.CreateTaskMsg` - Task created
- `tea.KeyMsg` - Keyboard input (largest section)
- `tasksUpdatedMsg` - Tasks updated
- `taskCreatedMsg` - Task created confirmation
- Various async operation messages

**Complexity factors**:
- Deeply nested switch statements (message type → key type)
- Keyboard handling has 30+ key cases
- State mutations scattered throughout
- Multiple component updates per handler
- Side effects (commands) interleaved with state updates

## Refactoring Strategy

### Phase 1: Extract Message Handlers

Create dedicated handler methods for each message type:

```go
// Update becomes a dispatcher
func (m *Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {
    case tea.WindowSizeMsg:
        return m.handleWindowSize(msg)
    case journalLoadedMsg:
        return m.handleJournalLoaded(msg)
    case journalUpdatedMsg:
        return m.handleJournalUpdated(msg)
    case tasksLoadedMsg:
        return m.handleTasksLoaded(msg)
    case tea.KeyMsg:
        return m.handleKeyPress(msg)
    // ... other message types
    default:
        return m, nil
    }
}

// Handler signature
// func (m *Model) handle<MessageType>(msg <MessageType>) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd)
```

**Benefits**:
- Each handler is focused and testable
- Clear separation of concerns
- Easier to understand message flow
- Simpler to add new message types

### Phase 2: Extract Keyboard Handlers

Break down keyboard handling by context/mode:

```go
func (m *Model) handleKeyPress(msg tea.KeyMsg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    key := msg.String()
    
    // Global keys (quit, help, etc.)
    if cmd := m.handleGlobalKeys(key); cmd != nil {
        return m, cmd
    }
    
    // Mode-specific keys
    switch m.mode {
    case ModeJournal:
        return m.handleJournalKeys(key)
    case ModeTasks:
        return m.handleTaskKeys(key)
    case ModeNotes:
        return m.handleNoteKeys(key)
    default:
        return m, nil
    }
}

func (m *Model) handleGlobalKeys(key string) tea.Cmd {
    switch key {
    case "q", "ctrl+c":
        return tea.Quit
    case "?":
        return m.showHelp()
    // ... other global keys
    }
    return nil
}

func (m *Model) handleJournalKeys(key string) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch key {
    case "h", "left":
        return m.handlePreviousDay()
    case "l", "right":
        return m.handleNextDay()
    case "i":
        return m.handleAddIntention()
    // ... journal-specific keys
    }
    return m, nil
}
```

**Benefits**:
- Keys organized by context
- Easy to see which keys are available in each mode
- Reduces nesting depth
- Simplifies testing of key handlers

### Phase 3: Extract Command Builders

Move command creation into separate methods:

```go
// Before: Inline command creation
case "d":
    if m.focusedPane == PaneTasks {
        taskID := m.getSelectedTaskID()
        return m, func() tea.Msg {
            // ... delete logic
        }
    }

// After: Dedicated command method
case "d":
    if m.focusedPane == PaneTasks {
        return m, m.deleteSelectedTask()
    }

func (m *Model) deleteSelectedTask() tea.Cmd {
    taskID := m.getSelectedTaskID()
    return func() tea.Msg {
        err := m.taskService.Delete(taskID)
        if err != nil {
            return taskDeletedMsg{error: err}
        }
        return taskDeletedMsg{taskID: taskID}
    }
}
```

**Benefits**:
- Commands are named and reusable
- Easier to test command logic
- Clear separation of UI logic and async operations
- Better error handling structure

## Acceptance Criteria

### 1. Handler Method Extraction

- [ ] Extract `handleWindowSize(tea.WindowSizeMsg)` 
- [ ] Extract `handleJournalLoaded(journalLoadedMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleJournalUpdated(journalUpdatedMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleTasksLoaded(tasksLoadedMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleTaskToggle(components.TaskToggleMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleDeleteTask(components.DeleteTaskMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleCreateTask(components.CreateTaskMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleTaskCreated(taskCreatedMsg)`
- [ ] Extract `handleTasksUpdated(tasksUpdatedMsg)`
- [ ] Extract handlers for all other custom message types

### 2. Keyboard Handler Extraction

- [ ] Extract `handleKeyPress(tea.KeyMsg)` as dispatcher
- [ ] Extract `handleGlobalKeys(string)` for app-level keys
- [ ] Extract `handleJournalKeys(string)` for journal mode
- [ ] Extract `handleTaskKeys(string)` for task mode
- [ ] Extract `handleNoteKeys(string)` for notes mode (if exists)
- [ ] Extract `handleNavigationKeys(string)` for common navigation

### 3. Command Method Extraction

- [ ] Extract `loadJournal()` as command builder
- [ ] Extract `loadTasks()` as command builder
- [ ] Extract `toggleTask(taskID)` as command builder
- [ ] Extract `deleteSelectedTask()` as command builder
- [ ] Extract `createTask()` as command builder
- [ ] Extract other async operations as command builders

### 4. Update Function Simplification

Final `Update()` function should:
- [ ] Be under 50 lines
- [ ] Contain only message type dispatch
- [ ] Have no business logic
- [ ] Have no nested switches
- [ ] Have clear fallthrough to default

**Target structure**:
```go
func (m *Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {
    case tea.WindowSizeMsg:
        return m.handleWindowSize(msg)
    case journalLoadedMsg:
        return m.handleJournalLoaded(msg)
    case tea.KeyMsg:
        return m.handleKeyPress(msg)
    // ... 10-15 more cases
    default:
        return m, nil
    }
}
```

### 5. Code Organization

- [ ] Group related handlers together in file
- [ ] Add clear section comments (// Message Handlers, // Key Handlers, etc.)
- [ ] Maintain consistent handler signatures
- [ ] Use helper methods for repeated patterns
- [ ] Keep handlers focused (one responsibility)

### 6. Testing

- [ ] Unit tests for each message handler
- [ ] Unit tests for keyboard handlers
- [ ] Unit tests for command builders
- [ ] Verify no behavioral changes (regression tests)
- [ ] Test edge cases (nil checks, empty state)

### 7. Documentation

- [ ] Add godoc comments for each handler
- [ ] Document handler responsibilities
- [ ] Add examples for complex handlers
- [ ] Update inline comments for clarity

## Implementation Guidance

### File Organization

**Before**:
```
internal/tui/model.go (1429 lines)
  - Update() function (508 lines, line 237-744)
  - Helper methods
  - View rendering
```

**After**:
```
internal/tui/
  model.go              (~800 lines)
    - Model struct
    - Init()
    - Update() (30-50 lines) - dispatcher only
    - View()
  handlers.go           NEW (~400 lines)
    - handleWindowSize()
    - handleJournalLoaded()
    - handleTasksLoaded()
    - ... other message handlers
  keyboard.go           NEW (~300 lines)
    - handleKeyPress() - dispatcher
    - handleGlobalKeys()
    - handleJournalKeys()
    - handleTaskKeys()
    - ... key handler groups
  commands.go           NEW (~200 lines)
    - loadJournal()
    - loadTasks()
    - toggleTask()
    - deleteSelectedTask()
    - ... async command builders
```

**Benefits of file split**:
- model.go stays focused on core structure
- handlers.go contains message handling logic
- keyboard.go contains all keyboard interactions
- commands.go contains async operations
- Each file under 400 lines

### Handler Method Pattern

**Signature**:
```go
func (m *Model) handle<MessageType>(msg <MessageType>) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd)
```

**Template**:
```go
func (m *Model) handleJournalLoaded(msg journalLoadedMsg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    logger.Debug("handling journalLoadedMsg", "date", msg.journal.Date)
    
    // 1. Update model state
    m.currentJournal = &msg.journal
    
    // 2. Update components
    if m.intentionList != nil {
        m.intentionList.UpdateIntentions(msg.journal.Intentions)
    }
    
    // 3. Return model and optional command
    return m, nil
}
```

**Guidelines**:
- Keep handlers under 50 lines
- Extract helper methods if logic is complex
- Log at entry for debugging
- Group related state updates
- Return early for error cases

### Testing Strategy

**Message handler tests**:
```go
func TestHandleJournalLoaded(t *testing.T) {
    m := &Model{}
    msg := journalLoadedMsg{
        journal: Journal{
            Date: time.Now(),
            Intentions: []Intention{{Text: "Test"}},
        },
    }
    
    updatedModel, cmd := m.handleJournalLoaded(msg)
    
    assert.NotNil(t, updatedModel.currentJournal)
    assert.Equal(t, 1, len(updatedModel.currentJournal.Intentions))
    assert.Nil(t, cmd)
}
```

**Keyboard handler tests**:
```go
func TestHandleJournalKeys(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        key         string
        expectCmd   bool
        expectState string
    }{
        {"h", true, "previous day"},
        {"l", true, "next day"},
        {"i", true, "add intention"},
    }
    
    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.key, func(t *testing.T) {
            m := &Model{mode: ModeJournal}
            _, cmd := m.handleJournalKeys(tt.key)
            if tt.expectCmd {
                assert.NotNil(t, cmd)
            }
        })
    }
}
```

- [ ] Test each handler in isolation
- [ ] Mock dependencies (services, components)
- [ ] Verify state changes
- [ ] Verify command creation
- [ ] Test error paths

### Incremental Refactoring Steps

**Step 1: Extract one message handler**
1. Create new file `internal/tui/handlers.go`
2. Extract `handleWindowSize()` as first handler
3. Update `Update()` to call it
4. Run tests, verify no changes
5. Commit

**Step 2: Extract remaining message handlers**
1. Extract handlers one by one
2. Test after each extraction
3. Commit after each handler or small batch

**Step 3: Extract keyboard handlers**
1. Create new file `internal/tui/keyboard.go`
2. Extract `handleKeyPress()` dispatcher
3. Extract `handleGlobalKeys()`
4. Extract mode-specific handlers
5. Test and commit

**Step 4: Extract command builders**
1. Create new file `internal/tui/commands.go`
2. Extract async command functions
3. Test and commit

**Step 5: Cleanup**
1. Remove old Update() implementation
2. Verify all tests pass
3. Run full integration test
4. Update documentation

### Migration Path

To avoid breaking changes, use this approach:

1. **Parallel implementation**: Add new handlers without removing old Update()
2. **Gradual migration**: Move one message type at a time
3. **Testing**: Verify each step with tests
4. **Feature flag** (optional): Allow switching between old/new implementation for testing
5. **Final cutover**: Remove old implementation once all handlers migrated

## Edge Cases to Handle

1. **Nil checks**: Ensure components exist before updating
2. **Mode transitions**: Handle state cleanup when switching modes
3. **Concurrent updates**: Ensure commands don't race
4. **Error propagation**: Maintain error handling through refactor
5. **State consistency**: Ensure model state remains valid after handler
6. **Component lifecycle**: Handle component creation/destruction properly

## Performance Considerations

- Refactoring should not impact performance (pure code organization)
- Avoid extra allocations in hot paths
- Keep handler dispatch fast (simple switch)
- Don't add abstraction overhead

## Definition of Done

- [ ] Update() function is under 50 lines
- [ ] All message handlers extracted to dedicated methods
- [ ] Keyboard handlers organized by mode/context
- [ ] Async commands extracted to separate functions
- [ ] Code split into logical files (handlers.go, keyboard.go, commands.go)
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] New unit tests for handlers (>80% coverage)
- [ ] No behavioral changes (regression tests pass)
- [ ] Code review completed
- [ ] Documentation updated (godoc, inline comments)
- [ ] Manual TUI testing confirms functionality preserved

## Time Estimate
6-8 hours (includes careful refactoring, testing, and documentation)

## Success Criteria

**Before**:
- 1 file, 1429 lines
- Update() function: 508 lines
- Difficult to understand and modify
- Hard to test individual handlers

**After**:
- 4 files, ~1700 lines total (slightly more due to function signatures)
- Update() function: 30-50 lines (dispatcher only)
- Clear separation of concerns
- Easy to test handlers in isolation
- Easy to add new message types or keys

## References
- Bubbletea best practices: https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/tree/master/examples
- Go refactoring patterns: Extract Method, Extract Class
- Clean Code principles: Single Responsibility, Small Functions
close_reason: Refactored 508-line Update() function into focused handlers across handlers.go, keyboard.go, and commands.go. Fixed all closure capture bugs and state mutation issues identified in review. All tests pass. Production-ready.

title: Add 'rk note new' CLI command for standalone notes
description:
## Overview
Implement the `rk note new` CLI command to create standalone zettelkasten notes. This is the primary way users create notes from the command line, with support for interactive editing, tags, and automatic slug generation.

## Background Context
- Notes are standalone markdown files stored in `~/.reckon/notes/`
- Each note has a unique slug (URL-friendly identifier)
- Notes can have tags for categorization
- Content is edited in user's \$EDITOR
- Must integrate with schema from reckon-63n (notes table, note_links table)
- Link detection will be handled by reckon-4pn integration

## Acceptance Criteria

### 1. Command Interface

**Basic usage**:
```bash
rk note new "My Note Title"
```

**With options**:
```bash
rk note new "My Note Title" --tags productivity,learning
rk note new "Quick Thought" --tag idea
rk note new --title "Note Title"  # Alternative flag
```

**Flags**:
- `--tags` / `-t` - Comma-separated list of tags
- `--tag` - Single tag (can be repeated: `--tag foo --tag bar`)
- `--editor` / `-e` - Override \$EDITOR
- `--no-edit` - Create with empty content (skip editor)
- `--template` - Use a template file

**Command structure**:
```
rk note new [TITLE] [flags]
```

- [ ] Accept title as positional argument or `--title` flag
- [ ] Support `--tags` and `--tag` flags
- [ ] Support `--editor` to override \$EDITOR
- [ ] Support `--no-edit` to skip editor
- [ ] Support `--template` for template files

### 2. Slug Generation

Generate URL-friendly slug from title:

**Rules**:
- Lowercase all characters
- Replace spaces with hyphens
- Remove special characters except hyphens
- Remove leading/trailing hyphens
- Truncate to 100 characters max
- Handle collisions by appending `-2`, `-3`, etc.

**Examples**:
```
"My Note Title"           → "my-note-title"
"Using C++ Effectively"   → "using-c-effectively"
"What's Next?"            → "whats-next"
"Ideas for 2026"          → "ideas-for-2026"
"note"                    → "note"
"note" (collision)        → "note-2"
```

**Implementation**:
```go
func GenerateSlug(title string) string {
    // Convert to lowercase
    slug := strings.ToLower(title)
    
    // Replace spaces with hyphens
    slug = strings.ReplaceAll(slug, " ", "-")
    
    // Remove special chars (keep alphanumeric and hyphens)
    slug = regexp.MustCompile(`[^a-z0-9-]+`).ReplaceAllString(slug, "")
    
    // Remove consecutive hyphens
    slug = regexp.MustCompile(`-+`).ReplaceAllString(slug, "-")
    
    // Trim hyphens
    slug = strings.Trim(slug, "-")
    
    // Truncate to 100 chars
    if len(slug) > 100 {
        slug = slug[:100]
        slug = strings.TrimRight(slug, "-")
    }
    
    return slug
}

func EnsureUniqueSlug(db *gorm.DB, slug string) (string, error) {
    uniqueSlug := slug
    counter := 2
    
    for {
        var count int64
        db.Model(&models.Note{}).Where("slug = ?", uniqueSlug).Count(&count)
        if count == 0 {
            return uniqueSlug, nil
        }
        uniqueSlug = fmt.Sprintf("%s-%d", slug, counter)
        counter++
    }
}
```

- [ ] Implement `GenerateSlug` function
- [ ] Implement `EnsureUniqueSlug` to handle collisions
- [ ] Validate slug is not empty after generation
- [ ] Use consistent slug generation across CLI and TUI

### 3. Note File Creation

**File location**: `~/.reckon/notes/<slug>.md`

**File format**:
```markdown
---
title: "My Note Title"
slug: "my-note-title"
created_at: 2026-01-18T10:30:00Z
updated_at: 2026-01-18T10:30:00Z
tags: ["productivity", "learning"]
---

[Content edited by user goes here]
```

**Workflow**:
1. Generate slug from title
2. Ensure slug is unique
3. Create note file with frontmatter
4. Open in \$EDITOR for user to write content
5. Parse file after editor closes
6. Save to database
7. Extract and save wiki links (integration with reckon-4pn)

- [ ] Create file in `~/.reckon/notes/` directory
- [ ] Write frontmatter with title, slug, timestamps, tags
- [ ] Open in \$EDITOR for content editing
- [ ] Parse frontmatter and content after editor closes
- [ ] Save to database with all fields
- [ ] Extract and store wiki links automatically

### 4. Editor Integration

**Editor selection** (priority order):
1. `--editor` flag
2. `\$EDITOR` environment variable
3. `\$VISUAL` environment variable
4. Default: `vi`

**Editor invocation**:
```go
func OpenEditor(filePath string, editor string) error {
    if editor == "" {
        editor = os.Getenv("EDITOR")
    }
    if editor == "" {
        editor = os.Getenv("VISUAL")
    }
    if editor == "" {
        editor = "vi"
    }
    
    cmd := exec.Command(editor, filePath)
    cmd.Stdin = os.Stdin
    cmd.Stdout = os.Stdout
    cmd.Stderr = os.Stderr
    
    return cmd.Run()
}
```

**Skip editor** (--no-edit):
- Create file with empty content
- Useful for scripting or creating placeholder notes

- [ ] Respect \$EDITOR environment variable
- [ ] Fall back to vi if not set
- [ ] Support `--editor` flag to override
- [ ] Support `--no-edit` to skip editor
- [ ] Handle editor errors gracefully

### 5. Template Support

Allow users to start from a template:

```bash
rk note new "Meeting Notes" --template ~/.reckon/templates/meeting.md
```

**Template file example**:
```markdown
---
title: "{{TITLE}}"
slug: "{{SLUG}}"
created_at: {{CREATED_AT}}
updated_at: {{UPDATED_AT}}
tags: {{TAGS}}
---

## Attendees
- 

## Agenda
- 

## Notes
- 

## Action Items
- [ ] 
```

**Template variables**:
- `{{TITLE}}` - Replaced with note title
- `{{SLUG}}` - Replaced with generated slug
- `{{CREATED_AT}}` - Replaced with timestamp
- `{{UPDATED_AT}}` - Replaced with timestamp
- `{{TAGS}}` - Replaced with tags array

- [ ] Support `--template` flag with file path
- [ ] Replace template variables with actual values
- [ ] Fall back to default template if file not found
- [ ] Document template format

### 6. Tag Handling

**Tag format**:
- Lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens
- Stored as JSON array in frontmatter and database
- Multiple tags supported

**Examples**:
```bash
rk note new "Note" --tags "foo,bar,baz"
rk note new "Note" --tag foo --tag bar --tag baz
```

**Tag normalization**:
```go
func NormalizeTags(tags []string) []string {
    normalized := make([]string, 0, len(tags))
    for _, tag := range tags {
        // Lowercase, trim spaces
        tag = strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(tag))
        // Skip empty
        if tag == "" {
            continue
        }
        normalized = append(normalized, tag)
    }
    return normalized
}
```

- [ ] Parse comma-separated tags from `--tags`
- [ ] Support multiple `--tag` flags
- [ ] Normalize tags (lowercase, trim)
- [ ] Store as JSON array in frontmatter: `["tag1", "tag2"]`
- [ ] Store in database `tags` column

### 7. Database Integration

**Service layer** (`internal/service/note_service.go`):

```go
func (s *NoteService) Create(note *models.Note, content string) error {
    // Start transaction
    tx := s.db.Begin()
    defer tx.Rollback()
    
    // Save note to database
    if err := tx.Create(note).Error; err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("failed to create note: %w", err)
    }
    
    // Write markdown file
    if err := s.writeNoteFile(note, content); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("failed to write note file: %w", err)
    }
    
    // Extract and save wiki links (from reckon-4pn)
    if err := s.UpdateNoteLinks(note.ID, content); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("failed to update links: %w", err)
    }
    
    // Resolve any orphaned backlinks to this note
    if err := s.ResolveOrphanedBacklinks(note.Slug, note.ID); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("failed to resolve backlinks: %w", err)
    }
    
    return tx.Commit().Error
}
```

- [ ] Create Note record in database
- [ ] Write markdown file to `~/.reckon/notes/`
- [ ] Extract and save wiki links (requires reckon-4pn)
- [ ] Resolve orphaned backlinks
- [ ] Use transaction for atomicity

### 8. Output & Feedback

**Success output**:
```
Created note: my-note-title
Location: ~/.reckon/notes/my-note-title.md
Links found: 3 (atomic-notes, permanent-notes, slip-box)
```

**Verbose output** (optional `--verbose`):
```
Generated slug: my-note-title
Created file: /home/user/.reckon/notes/my-note-title.md
Opening editor: vim
Saved to database (ID: 42)

  → atomic-notes
  → permanent-notes
  → slip-box
```

**Error messages**:
```
Error: title cannot be empty
Error: failed to open editor: vim not found
Error: note with slug 'my-note' already exists
```

- [ ] Print success message with slug and location
- [ ] Show number of links found (if any)
- [ ] Support `--verbose` for detailed output
- [ ] Clear error messages for common failures

### 9. Validation

**Before creating**:
- Title must not be empty
- Slug must not be empty after generation
- \$EDITOR must be available (unless --no-edit)
- `~/.reckon/notes/` directory must exist

**After editing**:
- File must exist
- Frontmatter must be valid YAML
- Title and slug must match database

- [ ] Validate title is not empty
- [ ] Validate slug is unique
- [ ] Validate editor is available
- [ ] Validate notes directory exists
- [ ] Validate frontmatter after editing

## Implementation Guidance

### File Structure
```
cmd/
  note/
    new.go            # NEW: 'rk note new' command
internal/
  service/
    note_service.go   # NEW: NoteService with Create method
  parser/

```

### Cobra Command

**File**: `cmd/note/new.go`

```go

    Use:   "new [TITLE]",
    Short: "Create a new standalone note",
    Long: `Create a new zettelkasten note with the given title.
    
The note will be created in ~/.reckon/notes/ and opened in your
\$EDITOR for content editing. After saving, the note is stored in
the database and any wiki-style links are extracted.`,

func init() {

    newCmd.Flags().StringSliceP("tags", "t", []string{}, "Comma-separated tags")
```

### Testing Requirements

**Unit tests**:

```go
    tests := []struct {
```

### Edge Cases

2. **Title with only special chars**: "!!!" → empty slug → error

## Example Usage
